title: US Data Center Siting Analysis
text:
This report presents a structural analysis of the county-level factors that drive data center siting decisions across all 3,138 US counties. Rather than relying on ad-hoc heuristics, we build a two-part hurdle model (LightGBM) that separately answers two questions:

What makes a county likely to attract any data center at all? (Stage 1 — Presence)
Among counties with data centers, what drives the scale of that presence? (Stage 2 — Scale)

This decomposition is methodologically important: the factors that determine whether a data center exists often differ from those that determine how many exist in a given market.






Note



Live Dashboard: An interactive version of the results is available at the Streamlit dashboard.

title: US Data Center Siting Analysis
text:
This report presents a structural analysis of the county-level factors that drive data center siting decisions across all 3,138 US counties. Rather than relying on ad-hoc heuristics, we build a two-part hurdle model (LightGBM) that separately answers two questions:

What makes a county likely to attract any data center at all? (Stage 1 — Presence)
Among counties with data centers, what drives the scale of that presence? (Stage 2 — Scale)

This decomposition is methodologically important: the factors that determine whether a data center exists often differ from those that determine how many exist in a given market.






Note



Live Dashboard: An interactive version of the results is available at the Streamlit dashboard.

title: US Data Center Siting Analysis
text:
Target Variable
Data center counts follow a highly zero-inflated distribution — 75.6% of US counties have no data centers at all. This is the central modeling challenge that motivates the hurdle approach over a simple regression.


                            
                                            
Distribution of data center counts across 3,138 US counties (log scale on y-axis).

title: US Data Center Siting Analysis
text:
Top Counties by Attractiveness Score
The model assigns each county an attractiveness score = P(presence) × E[scale | present]. This reflects both the probability of a county attracting any data center and the expected intensity of that presence.


                            
                                            
Top 20 counties ranked by model attractiveness score.

title: US Data Center Siting Analysis
text:
Key Findings (Preview)







Finding
Detail




Power infrastructure dominates
clean_energy_jobs and grid_infrastructure_jobs are the top two presence predictors — serving as proxies for grid capacity and power sector depth


IT labor depth drives scale
wage_information rises from rank 4 (presence) to rank 1 (scale), revealing an agglomeration effect among established markets


Hazard indices are geographic confounders
NRI risk scores appear in the top 8 presence predictors but drop to ranks 20–30 in scale — confirming they reflect where the existing market is, not causal attractors


Electricity cost matters more at scale
commercial_price rises from rank 9 to rank 4 between stages — cost sensitivity only becomes significant once a cluster exists



See the Interpretation page for full SHAP analysis.

title: Emerging Markets
text:
An emerging market in this context is a county that:

Has zero current data centers (no incumbents to compete with)
Has high structural attractiveness — the model finds the prerequisite conditions for DC activity already in place

These are first-mover opportunities: the infrastructure is there, the labor exists, the grid capacity is present — but no operator has yet planted a flag. The model identifies these by the gap between structural readiness and realized investment.


Zero-DC counties: 2,371
Zero-DC with P(presence) ≥ 0.50: 50  (2.1% of zero-DC)
Zero-DC with P(presence) ≥ 0.75: 32
Zero-DC with P(presence) ≥ 0.90: 16

title: Emerging Markets
section: P(presence) vs Expected Scale — The Opportunity Matrix
text:
P(presence) vs Expected Scale — The Opportunity Matrix
Not all emerging markets are equal. The two-part model lets us distinguish:

High P, High Scale: structurally ready and likely to see significant activity
High P, Low Scale: likely to attract some activity but probably small deployments
Low P, High Scale: rare — unlikely to see activity despite high potential scale



                            
                                            
Emerging market opportunity matrix. Point size = attractiveness score. Only zero-DC counties shown.

title: Emerging Markets
text:
Investment Thesis Interpretation
Three distinct opportunity profiles emerge from the top emerging markets:
Profile 1 — Metro Adjacency (e.g., New Hanover NC, Monmouth NJ) Counties adjacent to established DC markets that have absorbed surrounding infrastructure development (grid, fiber, labor) without yet attracting operators. These are likely to see activity as primary markets become capacity-constrained.
Profile 2 — Underserved Regional Hubs (e.g., Mercer NJ, Clackamas OR) Mid-size metros with strong structural foundations but no historical DC presence. Often offer lower land costs than primary markets with comparable grid and labor depth.
Profile 3 — Policy-Driven Opportunities (e.g., Sussex DE, Calvert MD) Counties in states with favorable data center tax incentives and regulatory environments. The policy signal (policy_direction_score) contributes positively to attractiveness even where other structural factors are more moderate.






Note



Limitation: This model captures structural conditions but cannot account for announced but not-yet-built capacity, utility queue positions, or off-market land deals. Real investment decisions require augmenting these signals with proprietary market intelligence.

title: Model Interpretation
text:
We use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) with TreeExplainer for exact SHAP values — not approximations. SHAP assigns each feature a contribution to the model’s output for each individual county, with the sum of contributions equaling the difference between the county’s prediction and the global mean prediction.

f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j

where \phi_0 is the base value (mean prediction) and \phi_j is feature j’s SHAP contribution. Positive SHAP → pushes prediction up; negative SHAP → pushes down.
All 3,138 county SHAP values were pre-computed and stored for efficient lookup.

title: Model Interpretation
text:
We use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) with TreeExplainer for exact SHAP values — not approximations. SHAP assigns each feature a contribution to the model’s output for each individual county, with the sum of contributions equaling the difference between the county’s prediction and the global mean prediction.

f(x) = \phi_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j

where \phi_0 is the base value (mean prediction) and \phi_j is feature j’s SHAP contribution. Positive SHAP → pushes prediction up; negative SHAP → pushes down.
All 3,138 county SHAP values were pre-computed and stored for efficient lookup.

title: Model Interpretation
text:
Key Interpretive Findings

1. Power Infrastructure as the Entry Gate
clean_energy_jobs (rank 1, S1) and grid_infrastructure_jobs (rank 2, S1) are the two strongest presence predictors. These are not directly measuring power availability but rather the depth of the power sector workforce — a proxy for grid capacity, interconnection maturity, and the density of utility infrastructure needed to serve a hyperscale campus.
This aligns with industry reporting: power access (not just price) is consistently cited as the top constraint on new data center development.


2. The Agglomeration Signal in wage_information
wage_information — the average wage in information-sector occupations — rises from rank 4 in Stage 1 to rank 1 in Stage 2. This is the clearest agglomeration signal in the model:

At the presence stage, IT labor is important but secondary to power infrastructure
At the scale stage, it becomes the dominant factor — established markets attract more facilities because they’ve already built deep IT talent pools

This is consistent with the “tech cluster” dynamic: once a few large operators anchor in a metro, the labor market deepens, which attracts more operators, reinforcing the cluster.


3. Geographic Confounding in Hazard Risk Indices
NRI hazard risk indices (lightning, hurricane, tornado, earthquake) appear in the top 8 Stage 1 predictors but fall to ranks 20–30 in Stage 2. This pattern is diagnostic of geographic confounding:
The existing US data center market is concentrated in Virginia (high lightning risk), Texas (hurricane/tornado risk), and Georgia (coastal storm risk). The model learns this geographic correlation in Stage 1. But once we condition on positive counties in Stage 2, the hazard indices lose predictive power — confirming they are proxies for where the market already is, not genuine attractors.






Important



Policy implication: Hazard risk should not be used as a site selection criterion based on this model. Counties with lower hazard risk are not inherently less attractive — the observed correlation reflects historical accident, not structural causation.




4. Electricity Cost Sensitivity at Scale
commercial_price rises from rank 9 (S1) to rank 4 (S2). Cost sensitivity is a scale-dependent phenomenon: small operators may accept above-market power costs if other factors (labor, land, connectivity) are favorable, but hyperscale campuses consuming 100s of MW are acutely sensitive to cents-per-kWh differences.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
The target variable — county-level data center count — violates the assumptions of standard regression in two compounding ways:

Zero-inflation: 75.6% of counties have exactly zero data centers. A regression trained on all counties conflates two fundamentally different groups: counties that could attract a DC but haven’t, and counties that are structurally unsuitable.
Right skew + overdispersion: Among positive counties, counts range from <1 to 304, with variance far exceeding the mean. Standard OLS residuals would be severely heteroscedastic.

These two problems motivate different solutions, which led us to evaluate two model families.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
The target variable — county-level data center count — violates the assumptions of standard regression in two compounding ways:

Zero-inflation: 75.6% of counties have exactly zero data centers. A regression trained on all counties conflates two fundamentally different groups: counties that could attract a DC but haven’t, and counties that are structurally unsuitable.
Right skew + overdispersion: Among positive counties, counts range from <1 to 304, with variance far exceeding the mean. Standard OLS residuals would be severely heteroscedastic.

These two problems motivate different solutions, which led us to evaluate two model families.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
Option 1 — Tweedie Regression
The Tweedie distribution with power parameter p ∈ (1, 2) is a compound Poisson-Gamma that naturally handles zero-inflation and overdispersion in a single model. It directly models E[y] with a log link, treating zeros as a structural outcome.
Key hyperparameter: the Tweedie power p, tuned via cross-validation.


                            
                                            


Best p = 1.3 (closer to Poisson than Gamma), reflecting that the zero mass is the dominant challenge over heavy-tail scaling.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
Option 2 — Two-Part Hurdle Model ✓
The hurdle model explicitly separates the two questions:

E[y] = \underbrace{P(y > 0)}_{\text{Stage 1}} \times \underbrace{E[y \mid y > 0]}_{\text{Stage 2}}

Stage 1 — Binary Classifier (LightGBM, is_unbalance=True): Predicts whether a county has any data center presence. Trained on all 3,138 counties.
Stage 2 — Tweedie Regressor (LightGBM, Tweedie p tuned): Predicts the expected count given presence. Trained only on the 767 counties with at least one data center.
This decomposition is both statistically principled and interpretively richer: we get separate SHAP explanations for the presence decision and the scale decision.

from lightgbm import LGBMClassifier, LGBMRegressor

# Stage 1 — presence
stage1 = LGBMClassifier(
    n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=0.05, num_leaves=63,
    is_unbalance=True,          # handles 76% zero class imbalance
    early_stopping_rounds=50,
)
stage1.fit(X_train, y_binary_train, eval_set=[(X_val, y_binary_val)])

# Stage 2 — scale (positive counties only)
stage2 = LGBMRegressor(
    objective="tweedie", tweedie_variance_power=best_p,
    n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=0.05, num_leaves=63,
    early_stopping_rounds=50,
)
stage2.fit(X_pos_train, y_pos_train, eval_set=[(X_pos_val, y_pos_val)])


Hyperparameter Tuning
Both stages used Optuna for Bayesian hyperparameter optimization (50 trials each), with 5-fold cross-validation as the objective. Key parameters tuned: num_leaves, learning_rate, min_child_samples, subsample, colsample_bytree, and reg_lambda. Stage 2 also tuned the Tweedie power p.
Stage 2 best p = 1.8 (closer to Gamma), reflecting that among positive counties, the count distribution is heavy-tailed — a handful of markets dominate the landscape.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
Model Comparison


                            
                                            
Performance comparison across five metrics. Option 2 (Hurdle) wins on four of five.











Option 1 — Tweedie
Option 2 — Hurdle ✓
Winner


Metric







Tweedie Deviance ↓
1.756
1.965
Tweedie


MAE log1p ↓
0.312
0.279
Hurdle ✓


RMSE log1p ↓
0.474
0.524
Tweedie


AUC ↑
0.807
0.833
Hurdle ✓


Median AE ↓
0.185
0.052
Hurdle ✓







The hurdle model wins on the metrics that matter most for practical application:

AUC +2.6 pp (0.807 → 0.833): better discrimination of DC-present vs absent counties
Median AE 3.6× lower (0.185 → 0.052): far more accurate predictions at the median, meaning the model is right more often for typical counties
MAE (log1p) improvement: better average accuracy in log space

The Tweedie model wins on Tweedie Deviance (expected, since it directly optimizes that objective) and RMSE (driven by a few large-count outliers that the Tweedie handles better in absolute terms). For siting decisions, discrimination and median accuracy are more actionable than extreme-tail RMSE.

title: Modeling Approach
text:
Why is_unbalance=True in Stage 1?
The binary classification target in Stage 1 is 76.2% negative (no DC present). Without correction, LightGBM would learn a trivial solution of predicting “no DC” for most counties. is_unbalance=True automatically adjusts class weights by the inverse frequency ratio, giving the minority class (has DC) proportionally more influence during training.
An alternative would be scale_pos_weight, but is_unbalance handles the ratio automatically and showed equivalent results in our cross-validation experiments.

title: Data Sources & Preprocessing
text:
Target Variable Construction
The target variable — number of data centers per county — was constructed by geocoding a proprietary database of US data center locations to county FIPS codes, then aggregating counts. Fractional counts arise from facilities that span county lines (allocated by area overlap).









Value


Statistic





Count
3,138


Mean
1.388


Median
0.000


Std Dev
8.462


Max
304.0


% Zero
75.6%


% > 10
3.3%


% > 100
0.2%





Target variable statistics. The extreme right skew motivates log1p transformation during modeling.

title: Data Sources & Preprocessing
text:
Feature Engineering
After merging all sources on county FIPS, 37 structural features were retained. Several features required non-trivial engineering choices:

BEA Data Suppression Floors
The Bureau of Economic Analysis suppresses small cell values to protect privacy. Three energy-related features are heavily affected:









Suppression Floor
% at Floor
Implication


Feature







epg_natural_gas
5
76.5%
Mostly uninformative — reflects suppression, n...


clean_energy_jobs
15
47.9%
Nearly half of counties are at the floor


grid_infrastructure_jobs
20
15.9%
Moderate suppression — still useful for rankings







Suppression indicator flags (*_above_floor) were added as binary features to allow the model to distinguish true low values from suppressed ones. This means SHAP values for these features should be interpreted carefully — a negative SHAP is often driven by the county being at the suppression floor rather than genuinely having low activity.


Electricity Price Aggregation
EIA electricity prices are reported at the utility zip code level. The pipeline aggregates to county level by:

Mapping zip codes to counties via the ZCTA crosswalk
Computing a population-weighted average for counties with multiple utility territories
Separately retaining commercial (commercial_price) and industrial (industrial_price) rates



Policy Direction Score
State-level data center policy stances were scraped from legislative databases and classified on a scale of −1 (hostile) to +1 (supportive) using GPT-4 assistance, then validated manually. This feature is assigned uniformly to all counties within a state.

title: Data Sources & Preprocessing
text:
Feature Correlation Structure


                            
                                            
Pearson correlation matrix for a subset of key structural features.


Notable correlations:

clean_energy_jobs and grid_infrastructure_jobs are strongly correlated (r ≈ 0.75) — both capture power sector depth and will compete for SHAP attribution.
wage_information correlates moderately with num_datacenters (r ≈ 0.45) — the agglomeration signal visible even in raw correlations.
Electricity prices (commercial_price, industrial_price) show weak raw correlation with DC counts, but emerge as important at scale in the Stage 2 model.

title: Data Sources & Preprocessing
text:
Preprocessing Pipeline

# Simplified view of the preprocessing steps
df = merge_all_sources(on="county_fips")   # left-join across 7 sources
df = drop_counties_with_missing_core(df)   # requires grid + land price data
df = add_suppression_flags(df)             # *_above_floor binary indicators
df = log1p_transform(df, skewed_cols)      # right-skewed count features
df = standardize(df, price_cols)           # z-score for price features
df = fill_missing(df, strategy="median")   # remaining NaN → column median

The final preprocessed dataset contains {n_counties:,} counties × {n_feats} features before modeling.


Final dataset: 3,138 counties × 37 features
Target range: [0.0, 304.0]
Positive rate: 24.4%
